1:
"And this is the blessing, wherewith Moses the man of God blessed the children of Israel before his death." The witnesses note the title of the chapter — the dying blessing of Moses, the man of God, upon Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses is called "the man of God," raised up of God and eminently qualified by him with gifts for the work. The witnesses note that this blessing was given before his death. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the this is the blessing — the parting benediction; the Moses the man of God — his eminent calling; the blessed Israel — the tribes gathered; the before his death — the legacy of a faithful leader (Gen. 49:28).

The witnesses agree the verse sets the solemn occasion — the last words of Moses, "the man of God," blessing the tribes as he was about to die: like Jacob before him, the great mediator gathers his people for a parting benediction; the title "man of God" marks his eminent calling and his standing as God's own servant and prophet; the dying blessing shows the love of a faithful leader for the people he had borne so long, leaving them not with rebuke but with blessing; it teaches that the servants of God close their course with prayer and benediction for the church, "this is the blessing" the legacy of Moses to Israel (cf. Gen. 49:28).

So the witnesses make the verse the dying blessing: The witnesses note the this is the blessing: the parting benediction. The witnesses note the Moses the man of God: his eminent calling. The witnesses note the blessed Israel: the tribes gathered. The witnesses note the before his death: the legacy of a faithful leader (Gen. 49:28). The expositors observe the this is the blessing: the parting benediction. The expositors observe the Moses the man of God: his eminent calling. The expositors observe the blessed Israel: the tribes gathered. The expositors observe the before his death: the legacy of a faithful leader (Gen. 49:28). The believer is taught to mark the this is the blessing, the Moses the man of God, the blessed Israel, the before his death, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that Moses, the man of God, blessed Israel before his death — the parting benediction of a faithful leader, leaving his people with blessing, the legacy of the great mediator (Gen. 49:28).
2:
"And he said, The LORD came from Sinai, and rose up from Seir unto them; he shined forth from mount Paran, and he came with ten thousands of saints: from his right hand went a fiery law for them." The witnesses note the majesty of God's coming at Sinai — shining forth with ten thousands of saints and a fiery law. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses was in some things an eye and ear witness, and in others inspired by the Spirit, after observing the common blessing all enjoyed. The witnesses note that the LORD came from Sinai with ten thousands of saints and a fiery law from his right hand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the came from Sinai — the majesty of his appearing; the shined forth — from Paran in glory; the ten thousands of saints — the attending angels; the a fiery law — from his right hand (Heb. 12:22).

The witnesses agree the blessing opens with the glorious majesty of God's appearing to give the law: "the LORD came from Sinai... shined forth from mount Paran... with ten thousands of saints: from his right hand went a fiery law"; the splendour of Sinai — the attending angels, the fiery law — magnifies the holiness and grandeur of the God who chose Israel; the "ten thousands of saints" the New Testament reads of the angels present at the giving of the law (cf. Acts 7:53; Heb. 12:22); it teaches that the law came from a God of awful majesty, the same God who shines forth in glory the giver of Israel's blessing.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD came from Sinai: The witnesses note the came from Sinai: the majesty of his appearing. The witnesses note the shined forth: from Paran in glory. The witnesses note the ten thousands of saints: the attending angels. The witnesses note the a fiery law: from his right hand (Heb. 12:22). The expositors observe the came from Sinai: the majesty of his appearing. The expositors observe the shined forth: from Paran in glory. The expositors observe the ten thousands of saints: the attending angels. The expositors observe the a fiery law: from his right hand (Heb. 12:22). The believer is taught to mark the came from Sinai, the shined forth, the ten thousands of saints, the a fiery law, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that the LORD came from Sinai with ten thousands of saints and a fiery law — the glorious majesty of God's appearing to give the law, the angels attending, the God of awful glory the giver of Israel's blessing (Heb. 12:22).
3:
"Yea, he loved the people; all his saints are in thy hand: and they sat down at thy feet; every one shall receive of thy words." The witnesses note God's love for his people, his saints held in his hand, sitting at his feet to receive his words. The witnesses note that Gill notes his giving the law in such a glorious manner was an instance of his love, a distinguishing blessing; how much more the love of God to his spiritual Israel. The witnesses note that all his saints are in his hand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he loved the people — the God of Sinai loving; the all his saints in thy hand — held perfectly safe; the sat down at thy feet — lowly to receive; the held secure — none plucking them out (John 10:28).

The witnesses agree the verse turns from majesty to love — the God of Sinai loving his people and holding them safe: "he loved the people; all his saints are in thy hand"; the keeping of the saints "in thy hand" marks their perfect security in God's grasp, and their sitting "at thy feet" their lowly readiness to receive his words; Gill raises it to the spiritual Israel, the deeper love of God to his own; it teaches that God's people are held safe in his hand, none able to pluck them thence, "neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand" (John 10:28), the loved disciples sitting at his feet to learn.

So the witnesses make the verse his saints in his hand: The witnesses note the he loved the people: the God of Sinai loving. The witnesses note the all his saints in thy hand: held perfectly safe. The witnesses note the sat down at thy feet: lowly to receive. The witnesses note the held secure: none plucking them out (John 10:28). The expositors observe the he loved the people: the God of Sinai loving. The expositors observe the all his saints in thy hand: held perfectly safe. The expositors observe the sat down at thy feet: lowly to receive. The expositors observe the held secure: none plucking them out (John 10:28). The believer is taught to mark the he loved the people, the all his saints in thy hand, the sat down at thy feet, the held secure, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that God loved his people and held his saints in his hand — the love of the God of Sinai, his own held safe, none plucking them from his hand, the loved sitting at his feet to receive his words (John 10:28).
4:
"Moses commanded us a law, even the inheritance of the congregation of Jacob." The witnesses note the law given through Moses as the inheritance of the congregation of Jacob. The witnesses note that Gill notes the law came from God, of his enacting, a declaration of his will, delivered to Moses and by him to the children of Israel. The witnesses note that it was the inheritance of the congregation. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the commanded us a law — enacted by God; the through Moses — the mediator; the the inheritance of Jacob — a precious heritage; the the word a treasure — taken as an heritage for ever (Ps. 119:111).

The witnesses agree the law itself is reckoned a precious heritage of God's people: "Moses commanded us a law, even the inheritance of the congregation of Jacob"; though enacted by God, it was delivered through Moses, and is called the people's "inheritance" — a treasure handed down to them and their children; the calling of the law an inheritance shows its worth, no burden but a possession to be valued and kept; it teaches that the word of God is the rich heritage of his church, "the testimonies... I have taken as an heritage for ever: for they are the rejoicing of my heart" (Ps. 119:111), the law a treasure given through the mediator.

So the witnesses make the verse the law an inheritance: The witnesses note the commanded us a law: enacted by God. The witnesses note the through Moses: the mediator. The witnesses note the the inheritance of Jacob: a precious heritage. The witnesses note the the word a treasure: taken as an heritage for ever (Ps. 119:111). The expositors observe the commanded us a law: enacted by God. The expositors observe the through Moses: the mediator. The expositors observe the the inheritance of Jacob: a precious heritage. The expositors observe the the word a treasure: taken as an heritage for ever (Ps. 119:111). The believer is taught to mark the commanded us a law, the through Moses, the the inheritance of Jacob, the the word a treasure, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that the law given through Moses is the inheritance of Jacob — the word of God a precious heritage, no burden but a treasure handed down, the testimonies taken as an heritage for ever (Ps. 119:111).
5:
"And he was king in Jeshurun, when the heads of the people and the tribes of Israel were gathered together." The witnesses note the LORD reigning as king in Jeshurun, when the people were gathered. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is God, as Jarchi — the government of Israel was a theocracy, their laws immediately from God, guided and directed by him. The witnesses note that he was king when the heads and tribes were gathered. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he was king — god himself reigning; the in Jeshurun — over upright Israel; the the people gathered — under his royal rule; the the King of the church — the LORD is our king (Isa. 33:22).

The witnesses agree the verse proclaims God himself the King of his people: "he was king in Jeshurun" — Israel a theocracy, ruled immediately by the LORD, their laws his and their guidance from him; the gathering of the heads and tribes marks the people under his royal rule; the divine kingship over Israel points to Christ, the King of his church, "the LORD is our king; he will save us" (Isa. 33:22); it teaches that God's people own no king but him, gathered under his rule, and that the church's true and only sovereign is the Lord who reigns over his own.

So the witnesses make the verse king in Jeshurun: The witnesses note the he was king: God himself reigning. The witnesses note the in Jeshurun: over upright Israel. The witnesses note the the people gathered: under his royal rule. The witnesses note the the King of the church: the LORD is our king (Isa. 33:22). The expositors observe the he was king: God himself reigning. The expositors observe the in Jeshurun: over upright Israel. The expositors observe the the people gathered: under his royal rule. The expositors observe the the King of the church: the LORD is our king (Isa. 33:22). The believer is taught to mark the he was king, the in Jeshurun, the the people gathered, the the King of the church, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that the LORD was king in Jeshurun — God himself the King of his people, ruling immediately over them, pointing to Christ the King of his church, the LORD our king who saves us (Isa. 33:22).
6:
"Let Reuben live, and not die; and let not his men be few." The witnesses note the blessing of Reuben — that the tribe live and not be extinct, though it should not excel. The witnesses note that Gill notes that Reuben should continue as a tribe and not be extinct, though not excel because of the sin of their progenitor; with special regard to their preservation beyond Jordan. The witnesses note that his men be not few. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the let Reuben live — the tribe to continue; the not die — not be extinct; the not excel — humbled for the father's sin; the mercy in judgment — in wrath remember mercy (Hab. 3:2).

The witnesses agree the blessing of Reuben is one of preservation rather than pre-eminence: "let Reuben live, and not die" — the tribe to continue, though, for the sin of its forefather, not to excel; the gracious wish for its survival shows mercy tempering the consequence of the father's sin, the tribe spared though humbled; it teaches that God remembers mercy even where sin has forfeited honour, preserving where he does not exalt, "in wrath remember mercy" (Hab. 3:2), Reuben kept alive by the grace that spares.

So the witnesses make the verse Reuben live: The witnesses note the let Reuben live: the tribe to continue. The witnesses note the not die: not be extinct. The witnesses note the not excel: humbled for the father's sin. The witnesses note the mercy in judgment: in wrath remember mercy (Hab. 3:2). The expositors observe the let Reuben live: the tribe to continue. The expositors observe the not die: not be extinct. The expositors observe the not excel: humbled for the father's sin. The expositors observe the mercy in judgment: in wrath remember mercy (Hab. 3:2). The believer is taught to mark the let Reuben live, the not die, the not excel, the mercy in judgment, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that Reuben should live and not die, though not excel — preservation rather than pre-eminence, mercy tempering the consequence of the father's sin, kept alive by sparing grace (Hab. 3:2).
7:
"And this is the blessing of Judah: and he said, Hear, LORD, the voice of Judah, and bring him unto his people: let his hands be sufficient for him; and be thou an help to him from his enemies." The witnesses note the blessing of Judah — that the LORD hear him, bring him to his people, and help him against his enemies. The witnesses note that Gill notes the blessing of Judah, the supplement made in the Targums. The witnesses note that his hands should be sufficient for him, and the LORD a help against his enemies. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the hear the voice of Judah — his prayer answered; the bring him to his people — returning in safety; the his hands sufficient — strength for the war; the an help from enemies — the LORD his helper (Ps. 118:6-7).

The witnesses agree the blessing of Judah is one of leadership, prayer heard, and help in war: "hear, LORD, the voice of Judah... let his hands be sufficient... be thou an help to him from his enemies"; Judah, the royal and warlike tribe, is given the promise of God's answer to his cry and strength against his foes; the tribe of David and of Christ is blessed with victory and divine aid; it teaches that God hears the cry of his people and is their help against all enemies, "the LORD is on my side... the LORD is my helper" (Ps. 118:6-7), Judah's blessing fulfilled in the Lion of his tribe.

So the witnesses make the verse the blessing of Judah: The witnesses note the hear the voice of Judah: his prayer answered. The witnesses note the bring him to his people: returning in safety. The witnesses note the his hands sufficient: strength for the war. The witnesses note the an help from enemies: the LORD his helper (Ps. 118:6-7). The expositors observe the hear the voice of Judah: his prayer answered. The expositors observe the bring him to his people: returning in safety. The expositors observe the his hands sufficient: strength for the war. The expositors observe the an help from enemies: the LORD his helper (Ps. 118:6-7). The believer is taught to mark the hear the voice of Judah, the bring him to his people, the his hands sufficient, the an help from enemies, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that Judah is blessed with prayer heard and help against his enemies — the royal, warlike tribe given God's answer and aid, fulfilled in the Lion of Judah, the LORD his helper (Ps. 118:6-7).
8:
"And of Levi he said, Let thy Thummim and thy Urim be with thy holy one, whom thou didst prove at Massah, and with whom thou didst strive at the waters of Meribah;" The witnesses note the blessing of Levi — the Urim and Thummim of the priesthood entrusted to the holy one. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses said of the tribe of Levi. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the Thummim and Urim with the holy one, proved at Massah and Meribah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Thummim and Urim — the priestly oracle; the with thy holy one — entrusted to the priest; the proved at Massah — the tribe tested; the a shadow of Christ — the holy High Priest (Heb. 7:26).

The witnesses agree the blessing of Levi centres on the priestly office and its sacred trust: "let thy Thummim and thy Urim be with thy holy one" — the means of seeking God's will entrusted to the priest, the "holy one" of the tribe; the recalling of Massah and Meribah marks both the testing of Levi and God's grace in giving them so high a charge despite it; the priestly office, with its Urim and Thummim, points to Christ our great High Priest, in whom dwell the perfections of light and truth; it teaches the dignity of the ministry that bears God's oracle, the priesthood a shadow of the perfect Priest (cf. Heb. 7:26, holy, harmless, undefiled).

So the witnesses make the verse the Urim and Thummim: The witnesses note the Thummim and Urim: the priestly oracle. The witnesses note the with thy holy one: entrusted to the priest. The witnesses note the proved at Massah: the tribe tested. The witnesses note the a shadow of Christ: the holy High Priest (Heb. 7:26). The expositors observe the Thummim and Urim: the priestly oracle. The expositors observe the with thy holy one: entrusted to the priest. The expositors observe the proved at Massah: the tribe tested. The expositors observe the a shadow of Christ: the holy High Priest (Heb. 7:26). The believer is taught to mark the Thummim and Urim, the with thy holy one, the proved at Massah, the a shadow of Christ, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that Levi is blessed with the Urim and Thummim, the priestly oracle — the dignity of the office that bears God's will, the priesthood a shadow of Christ our holy High Priest (Heb. 7:26).
9:
"Who said unto his father and to his mother, I have not seen him; neither did he acknowledge his brethren, nor knew his own children: for they have observed thy word, and kept thy covenant." The witnesses note Levi's faithfulness — putting God's cause above the nearest natural ties. The witnesses note that Gill notes some understand it of the high priests who were not to defile themselves for father or mother; others of having no respect to them in judgment, but determining justly. The witnesses note that they observed God's word and kept his covenant. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I have not seen him — no sparing of kindred; the nor knew his children — god's cause above flesh; the observed thy word — loyal to the covenant; the love to God first — above father and mother (Matt. 10:37).

The witnesses agree the blessing commends Levi's singular faithfulness in setting God's cause above the dearest natural bonds: they "said unto father and mother, I have not seen him," not sparing even kindred where God's honour was at stake — whether in the matter of the calf, or in impartial judgment; their loyalty to "thy word" and "thy covenant" above flesh and blood marks the spirit God requires of his servants; it teaches that love to God must come before love to the nearest kin, "he that loveth father or mother more than me is not worthy of me" (Matt. 10:37), Levi honoured for putting the covenant first.

So the witnesses make the verse God above kin: The witnesses note the I have not seen him: no sparing of kindred. The witnesses note the nor knew his children: God's cause above flesh. The witnesses note the observed thy word: loyal to the covenant. The witnesses note the love to God first: above father and mother (Matt. 10:37). The expositors observe the I have not seen him: no sparing of kindred. The expositors observe the nor knew his children: God's cause above flesh. The expositors observe the observed thy word: loyal to the covenant. The expositors observe the love to God first: above father and mother (Matt. 10:37). The believer is taught to mark the I have not seen him, the nor knew his children, the observed thy word, the love to God first, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that Levi set God's cause above the nearest ties, keeping his covenant — the faithfulness that puts love to God before the dearest kin, honoured for loving the covenant above flesh and blood (Matt. 10:37).
10:
"They shall teach Jacob thy judgments, and Israel thy law: they shall put incense before thee, and whole burnt sacrifice upon thine altar." The witnesses note the twofold office of Levi — to teach the law and to offer at the altar. The witnesses note that Gill notes the priests and Levites, dispersed among the tribes, taught the people the laws, statutes, and ordinances of the Lord. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the teaching and the offering. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the teach Jacob thy judgments — the instruction of the law; the Israel thy law — the people taught; the incense and sacrifice — the worship of the altar; the word and altar joined — the priest's lips keep knowledge (Mal. 2:7).

The witnesses agree the verse defines the double charge of Levi — instruction and worship: "they shall teach Jacob thy judgments... put incense before thee, and whole burnt sacrifice upon thine altar"; the priests were both the teachers of God's law to the people and the ministers of his altar, joining the word and the offering; both offices point to Christ, the great Teacher and the great Sacrifice, who instructs his people and offers himself; it teaches that the ministry serves God by making known his truth and leading his worship, "the priest's lips should keep knowledge" (Mal. 2:7), the word and the altar joined in their office.

So the witnesses make the verse teach and offer: The witnesses note the teach Jacob thy judgments: the instruction of the law. The witnesses note the Israel thy law: the people taught. The witnesses note the incense and sacrifice: the worship of the altar. The witnesses note the word and altar joined: the priest's lips keep knowledge (Mal. 2:7). The expositors observe the teach Jacob thy judgments: the instruction of the law. The expositors observe the Israel thy law: the people taught. The expositors observe the incense and sacrifice: the worship of the altar. The expositors observe the word and altar joined: the priest's lips keep knowledge (Mal. 2:7). The believer is taught to mark the teach Jacob thy judgments, the Israel thy law, the incense and sacrifice, the word and altar joined, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that Levi was to teach the law and offer at the altar — the double charge of word and worship, both pointing to Christ the Teacher and Sacrifice, the priest's lips keeping knowledge (Mal. 2:7).
11:
"Bless, LORD, his substance, and accept the work of his hands: smite through the loins of them that rise against him, and of them that hate him, that they rise not again." The witnesses note the prayer for Levi — that God bless his substance, accept his work, and smite his enemies. The witnesses note that Gill notes Levi's substance lay in tithes, firstfruits, and the cities and devoted things given them, having no share in the land. The witnesses note that God would accept their work and smite those who rose against them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the bless his substance — the provision of Levi; the accept the work — his service owned of God; the smite his enemies — the protection given; the God defends his ministers — touch not mine anointed (Ps. 105:15).

The witnesses agree the blessing on Levi asks both provision and protection: "bless, LORD, his substance, and accept the work of his hands... smite through the loins of them that rise against him"; the prayer that God "accept the work" marks the need that the ministry's service be owned and blessed by him, and the petition against their enemies the defence God gives his servants; it teaches that those who serve at God's altar are to be maintained and their work accepted, and that God will defend his ministers against all who oppose them, "touch not mine anointed, and do my prophets no harm" (Ps. 105:15).

So the witnesses make the verse bless and accept: The witnesses note the bless his substance: the provision of Levi. The witnesses note the accept the work: his service owned of God. The witnesses note the smite his enemies: the protection given. The witnesses note the God defends his ministers: touch not mine anointed (Ps. 105:15). The expositors observe the bless his substance: the provision of Levi. The expositors observe the accept the work: his service owned of God. The expositors observe the smite his enemies: the protection given. The expositors observe the God defends his ministers: touch not mine anointed (Ps. 105:15). The believer is taught to mark the bless his substance, the accept the work, the smite his enemies, the God defends his ministers, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that the prayer for Levi asks provision, acceptance, and protection — the ministry maintained, its work owned of God, defended against all who oppose, touch not mine anointed (Ps. 105:15).
12:
"And of Benjamin he said, The beloved of the LORD shall dwell in safety by him; and the LORD shall cover him all the day long, and he shall dwell between his shoulders." The witnesses note the blessing of Benjamin — the beloved dwelling in safety, covered by the LORD all day, borne between his shoulders. The witnesses note that Gill notes Benjamin is named next to Levi because the temple, where the priests officiated, was in or near his tribe. The witnesses note that the beloved should dwell in safety and be covered by the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the beloved of the LORD — benjamin near to God; the dwell in safety — under his covering; the cover him all day — constant protection; the between his shoulders — borne tenderly (Isa. 40:11).

The witnesses agree the blessing of Benjamin is one of peculiar nearness and safety in God: "the beloved of the LORD shall dwell in safety by him... the LORD shall cover him all the day long, and he shall dwell between his shoulders"; the figures — covered all day, borne "between his shoulders" as a father carries a child or a shepherd a lamb — speak of constant protection and tender nearness; the temple's nearness to Benjamin made him dwell, as it were, close to God; it teaches the security of those God loves, dwelling safe under his covering and carried on his shoulders, "he shall gather the lambs with his arm, and carry them in his bosom" (Isa. 40:11), the beloved kept all the day long.

So the witnesses make the verse the beloved in safety: The witnesses note the the beloved of the LORD: Benjamin near to God. The witnesses note the dwell in safety: under his covering. The witnesses note the cover him all day: constant protection. The witnesses note the between his shoulders: borne tenderly (Isa. 40:11). The expositors observe the the beloved of the LORD: Benjamin near to God. The expositors observe the dwell in safety: under his covering. The expositors observe the cover him all day: constant protection. The expositors observe the between his shoulders: borne tenderly (Isa. 40:11). The believer is taught to mark the the beloved of the LORD, the dwell in safety, the cover him all day, the between his shoulders, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that Benjamin, the beloved, dwells safe by the LORD, covered all day and borne between his shoulders — the security and tender nearness of those God loves, carried in his bosom (Isa. 40:11).
13:
"And of Joseph he said, Blessed of the LORD be his land, for the precious things of heaven, for the dew, and for the deep that coucheth beneath," The witnesses note the blessing of Joseph — his land enriched with the precious things of heaven and the deep. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses said of the tribe of Joseph. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the land blessed for the precious things of heaven, the dew, and the deep beneath. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the blessed be his land — the richest blessing; the precious things of heaven — the bounty of the sky; the the dew — the gift of rain; the the deep beneath — the springs and depths (Gen. 49:25).

The witnesses agree the blessing of Joseph is the richest of the temporal blessings, his land favoured above all: "blessed of the LORD be his land, for the precious things of heaven, for the dew, and for the deep that coucheth beneath" — the bounty of sky and spring and depth poured upon his portion; the heaping of precious things marks the abundance of God's favour to Joseph, recalling Jacob's like blessing (Gen. 49:25); it teaches that God enriches his people from heaven above and the deep beneath, the dew and the springs his gifts, every good thing coming down from the Father of lights (cf. James 1:17).

So the witnesses make the verse the precious things: The witnesses note the blessed be his land: the richest blessing. The witnesses note the precious things of heaven: the bounty of the sky. The witnesses note the the dew: the gift of rain. The witnesses note the the deep beneath: the springs and depths (Gen. 49:25). The expositors observe the blessed be his land: the richest blessing. The expositors observe the precious things of heaven: the bounty of the sky. The expositors observe the the dew: the gift of rain. The expositors observe the the deep beneath: the springs and depths (Gen. 49:25). The believer is taught to mark the blessed be his land, the precious things of heaven, the the dew, the the deep beneath, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that Joseph's land is blessed with the precious things of heaven and the deep — the richest of temporal blessings, God enriching his people from above and beneath, every good gift from the Father (James 1:17).
14:
"And for the precious fruits brought forth by the sun, and for the precious things put forth by the moon," The witnesses note the precious fruits ripened by sun and moon upon Joseph's land. The witnesses note that Gill notes the sun has a wonderful influence on the fruits of the earth, to produce, ripen, and perfect them; Jarchi says the land of Joseph lay open to the sun. The witnesses note that the moon also put forth its precious things. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the precious fruits by the sun — ripened by its heat; the the sun's influence — perfecting the harvest; the put forth by the moon — the seasons forwarding; the nature serving God's own — the moon for seasons (Ps. 104:19).

The witnesses agree the blessing extends to the influences of the heavenly lights upon the land's increase: "the precious fruits brought forth by the sun... put forth by the moon" — the sun ripening and the moon's seasons forwarding the fruits of Joseph's portion; the noting of sun and moon as God's instruments of fruitfulness magnifies how the whole order of nature serves to enrich his people; it teaches that God uses the courses of the heavens for the good of his own, the sun and moon ordered to bless the land, "he appointed the moon for seasons: the sun knoweth his going down" (Ps. 104:19), all working for the fruitful increase.

So the witnesses make the verse fruits of sun and moon: The witnesses note the precious fruits by the sun: ripened by its heat. The witnesses note the the sun's influence: perfecting the harvest. The witnesses note the put forth by the moon: the seasons forwarding. The witnesses note the nature serving God's own: the moon for seasons (Ps. 104:19). The expositors observe the precious fruits by the sun: ripened by its heat. The expositors observe the the sun's influence: perfecting the harvest. The expositors observe the put forth by the moon: the seasons forwarding. The expositors observe the nature serving God's own: the moon for seasons (Ps. 104:19). The believer is taught to mark the precious fruits by the sun, the the sun's influence, the put forth by the moon, the nature serving God's own, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that the precious fruits are ripened by sun and moon — the heavenly lights God's instruments of fruitfulness, the whole order of nature serving to enrich his people, the moon appointed for seasons (Ps. 104:19).
15:
"And for the chief things of the ancient mountains, and for the precious things of the lasting hills," The witnesses note the chief things of the ancient mountains and lasting hills among Joseph's blessings. The witnesses note that Gill notes the ancient mountains, from the beginning of the world, for which the land of Joseph — Manasseh and Ephraim — was famous, as Gilead, Bashan, and Mount Ephraim. The witnesses note that the lasting hills yielded their precious things. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the chief things — of the ancient mountains; the precious things — of the lasting hills; the Gilead and Ephraim — the famed heights; the abundant and enduring — the everlasting hills (Gen. 49:26).

The witnesses agree the blessing reaches to the rich produce of the mountains and hills of Joseph's portion: "the chief things of the ancient mountains... the precious things of the lasting hills" — the famed heights of Gilead, Bashan, and Ephraim, fruitful and enduring; the calling of the mountains "ancient" and the hills "lasting" marks the stability and antiquity of these blessings, fixed from of old; it teaches that God's gifts to his people are both abundant and enduring, the everlasting hills yielding their treasures, the blessing of Joseph reaching to "the everlasting hills" (cf. Gen. 49:26), stable as the mountains.

So the witnesses make the verse the ancient mountains: The witnesses note the chief things: of the ancient mountains. The witnesses note the precious things: of the lasting hills. The witnesses note the Gilead and Ephraim: the famed heights. The witnesses note the abundant and enduring: the everlasting hills (Gen. 49:26). The expositors observe the chief things: of the ancient mountains. The expositors observe the precious things: of the lasting hills. The expositors observe the Gilead and Ephraim: the famed heights. The expositors observe the abundant and enduring: the everlasting hills (Gen. 49:26). The believer is taught to mark the chief things, the precious things, the Gilead and Ephraim, the abundant and enduring, and to rest in the God of Jeshurun. The verse thus records that the ancient mountains and lasting hills yield their chief things to Joseph — God's gifts abundant and enduring, the everlasting hills yielding their treasures, stable as the mountains (Gen. 49:26).
16:
"And for the precious things of the earth and fulness thereof, and for the good will of him that dwelt in the bush: let the blessing come upon the head of Joseph..." The witnesses note the fulness of the earth and the good will of him that dwelt in the bush blessing Joseph. The witnesses note that Gill notes the corn, grass, and cattle of the earth, for which part of Joseph's land, Bashan, was famous. The witnesses note that the good will of him that dwelt in the bush was upon Joseph. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the precious things of the earth — its fulness; the the good will — of God himself; the him that dwelt in the bush — the I AM, the deliverer; the the best blessing — in his favour is life (Ps. 30:5).

The witnesses agree the crowning of Joseph's blessing is "the good will of him that dwelt in the bush" — the favour of the very God who appeared to Moses in the burning bush: above all the precious things of earth, the deepest blessing is God's own good pleasure resting on Joseph; the recalling of the bush, where God revealed himself as the I AM and the deliverer, grounds the blessing in his covenant grace; it teaches that the best of all blessings is the good will of God himself, "in his favour is life" (Ps. 30:5), the favour of him that dwelt in the bush better than all the fulness of the earth.

So the witnesses make the verse the good will of the bush: The witnesses note the precious things of the earth: its fulness. The witnesses note the the good will: of God himself. The witnesses note the him that dwelt in the bush: the I AM, the deliverer. The witnesses note the the best blessing: in his favour is life (Ps. 30:5). The expositors observe the precious things of the earth: its fulness. The expositors observe the the good will: of God himself. The expositors observe the him that dwelt in the bush: the I AM, the deliverer. The expositors observe the the best blessing: in his favour is life (Ps. 30:5). The believer is taught to mark the precious things of the earth, the the good will, the him that dwelt in the bush, the the best blessing, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that the good will of him that dwelt in the bush crowns Joseph's blessing — the favour of God himself the deepest blessing, better than all the earth's fulness, in his favour is life (Ps. 30:5).
17:
"His glory is like the firstling of his bullock, and his horns are like the horns of unicorns: with them he shall push the people together to the ends of the earth..." The witnesses note Joseph's strength and glory likened to a firstling bullock and the horns of the wild ox. The witnesses note that Gill notes the firstling of his bullock, such as were in Bashan, comely and majestic. The witnesses note that with his horns he should push the people to the ends of the earth — the ten thousands of Ephraim and thousands of Manasseh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the firstling bullock — his glory and majesty; the horns of unicorns — strength and victory; the push the people — the nations driven before; the strength to overcome — treading down by his name (Ps. 44:5).

The witnesses agree the blessing sets forth Joseph's power and the multitude of his seed: "his glory is like the firstling of his bullock, and his horns are like the horns of unicorns" — strength, majesty, and victorious force, pushing the nations before him; the figure of the strong young ox and the wild ox's horns marks the might of Ephraim and Manasseh, the ten thousands and thousands; it teaches that God gives his people strength to overcome, the horn a token of power and victory, "in thy name will we tread them under that rise up against us" (Ps. 44:5), Joseph mighty by God's blessing.

So the witnesses make the verse horns of the wild ox: The witnesses note the the firstling bullock: his glory and majesty. The witnesses note the horns of unicorns: strength and victory. The witnesses note the push the people: the nations driven before. The witnesses note the strength to overcome: treading down by his name (Ps. 44:5). The expositors observe the the firstling bullock: his glory and majesty. The expositors observe the horns of unicorns: strength and victory. The expositors observe the push the people: the nations driven before. The expositors observe the strength to overcome: treading down by his name (Ps. 44:5). The believer is taught to mark the the firstling bullock, the horns of unicorns, the push the people, the strength to overcome, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that Joseph's strength is like the bullock and the wild ox's horns — power and victory over the nations, God giving his people strength to overcome, treading down by his name (Ps. 44:5).
18:
"And of Zebulun he said, Rejoice, Zebulun, in thy going out; and, Issachar, in thy tents." The witnesses note the blessing of Zebulun and Issachar — joy in going out and in their tents. The witnesses note that Gill notes Zebulun and Issachar are joined, being brethren of the same mother; Zebulun the younger set before Issachar, as in Jacob's blessing. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the rejoicing of Zebulun abroad and Issachar at home. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rejoice, Zebulun — joy in going out; the in thy going out — the seafaring tribe; the Issachar in thy tents — joy at home; the every calling blessed — blessed coming in and going out (Deut. 28:6).

The witnesses agree the blessing of the two brother-tribes covers both their callings — abroad and at home: "rejoice, Zebulun, in thy going out; and, Issachar, in thy tents"; Zebulun, the seafaring and trading tribe, is given joy in its journeys, Issachar, the settled husbandman, joy in its dwellings; the pairing shows God's blessing reaching to every honest calling, the merchant and the farmer alike; it teaches that God gives gladness in our several labours and stations, whether we go out or abide, "thou shalt be blessed when thou comest in, and... when thou goest out" (Deut. 28:6), joy in the calling God assigns.

So the witnesses make the verse going out and tents: The witnesses note the rejoice, Zebulun: joy in going out. The witnesses note the in thy going out: the seafaring tribe. The witnesses note the Issachar in thy tents: joy at home. The witnesses note the every calling blessed: blessed coming in and going out (Deut. 28:6). The expositors observe the rejoice, Zebulun: joy in going out. The expositors observe the in thy going out: the seafaring tribe. The expositors observe the Issachar in thy tents: joy at home. The expositors observe the every calling blessed: blessed coming in and going out (Deut. 28:6). The believer is taught to mark the rejoice, Zebulun, the in thy going out, the Issachar in thy tents, the every calling blessed, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that Zebulun rejoiced in going out and Issachar in his tents — God's blessing reaching to every honest calling, joy in the labour and station he assigns, blessed coming in and going out (Deut. 28:6).
19:
"They shall call the people unto the mountain; there they shall offer sacrifices of righteousness: for they shall suck of the abundance of the seas, and of treasures hid in the sand." The witnesses note the two tribes calling the people to worship and enriched by the abundance of the seas. The witnesses note that Gill, with the Targums, notes the calling of the people to the mountain of the sanctuary, the temple Moses foresaw, where they would come up to worship. The witnesses note that they should suck the abundance of the seas and treasures hid in the sand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the call the people — to the mountain of worship; the sacrifices of righteousness — true worship offered; the abundance of the seas — enriched by trade; the gain sanctified to God — the abundance of the sea converted (Isa. 60:5).

The witnesses agree the blessing joins worship to wealth — the tribes calling others to God and themselves enriched from the sea: "they shall call the people unto the mountain... offer sacrifices of righteousness... suck of the abundance of the seas"; their prosperity in trade and the deep was to be sanctified, drawing themselves and others up to the house of God; the joining of gain and godliness shows that earthly increase is rightly used when it leads to worship; it teaches that the people of God are to consecrate their abundance to him and invite others to his praise, "they shall come and sing in the height of Zion... for the abundance of the sea shall be converted unto thee" (cf. Isa. 60:5).

So the witnesses make the verse worship and abundance: The witnesses note the call the people: to the mountain of worship. The witnesses note the sacrifices of righteousness: true worship offered. The witnesses note the abundance of the seas: enriched by trade. The witnesses note the gain sanctified to God: the abundance of the sea converted (Isa. 60:5). The expositors observe the call the people: to the mountain of worship. The expositors observe the sacrifices of righteousness: true worship offered. The expositors observe the abundance of the seas: enriched by trade. The expositors observe the gain sanctified to God: the abundance of the sea converted (Isa. 60:5). The believer is taught to mark the call the people, the sacrifices of righteousness, the abundance of the seas, the gain sanctified to God, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that the tribes called the people to worship and were enriched from the seas — gain sanctified to God, earthly abundance leading to his praise, the wealth of the sea brought to him (Isa. 60:5).
20:
"And of Gad he said, Blessed be he that enlargeth Gad: he dwelleth as a lion, and teareth the arm with the crown of the head." The witnesses note the blessing of Gad — enlarged by God, dwelling like a lion, valiant in war. The witnesses note that Clarke notes deliverance out of distress is called enlarging (Ps. 4:1), referring to God's delivering Gad. The witnesses note that he dwelt as a lion, tearing the arm and the crown of the head. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the blessed be he that enlargeth — god who gives room; the Gad — delivered from distress; the as a lion — valiant in war; the victory God's gift — thou hast enlarged me (Ps. 4:1).

The witnesses agree the blessing of Gad celebrates God's enlarging grace and the tribe's lion-like valour: "blessed be he that enlargeth Gad: he dwelleth as a lion"; the praise goes first to God who "enlargeth" — delivers and gives room — and then to Gad's courage in battle, fierce as a lion; Clarke notes that deliverance from distress is "enlarging" (Ps. 4:1); it teaches that all victory and increase are God's gift, the strength of his people from him who makes room for them, "thou hast enlarged me when I was in distress" (Ps. 4:1), Gad's might the fruit of God's blessing.

So the witnesses make the verse God enlargeth Gad: The witnesses note the blessed be he that enlargeth: God who gives room. The witnesses note the Gad: delivered from distress. The witnesses note the as a lion: valiant in war. The witnesses note the victory God's gift: thou hast enlarged me (Ps. 4:1). The expositors observe the blessed be he that enlargeth: God who gives room. The expositors observe the Gad: delivered from distress. The expositors observe the as a lion: valiant in war. The expositors observe the victory God's gift: thou hast enlarged me (Ps. 4:1). The believer is taught to mark the blessed be he that enlargeth, the Gad, the as a lion, the victory God's gift, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that God enlarges Gad, who dwells as a lion — all victory and increase God's gift, the strength of his people from him who makes room, thou hast enlarged me in distress (Ps. 4:1).
21:
"And he provided the first part for himself, because there, in a portion of the lawgiver, was he seated; and he came with the heads of the people, he executed the justice of the LORD..." The witnesses note Gad choosing the first part — the land of Sihon and Og — yet going armed with his brethren to execute God's justice. The witnesses note that Gill, with Jarchi, notes the first part was the portion of Sihon and Og, the firstfruits of the land subdued, which Gad chose for his inheritance. The witnesses note that he came with the heads of the people and executed the justice of the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the first part — sihon and Og's land; the chose for himself — his early inheritance; the came with the heads — going armed with his brethren; the self and duty joined — look on the things of others (Phil. 2:4).

The witnesses agree the blessing commends Gad both for his early portion and his faithful service: "he provided the first part for himself... yet came with the heads of the people, he executed the justice of the LORD"; though Gad took his inheritance early, east of Jordan, he did not therefore forsake his brethren but went armed before them to do God's will in the conquest; the joining of his own settlement to his service shows the right ordering of self-interest and duty; it teaches that the people of God are to bear their part in the common cause even when their own portion is secured, "look not every man on his own things, but every man also on the things of others" (Phil. 2:4), Gad faithful in the common warfare.

So the witnesses make the verse the first part and service: The witnesses note the the first part: Sihon and Og's land. The witnesses note the chose for himself: his early inheritance. The witnesses note the came with the heads: going armed with his brethren. The witnesses note the self and duty joined: look on the things of others (Phil. 2:4). The expositors observe the the first part: Sihon and Og's land. The expositors observe the chose for himself: his early inheritance. The expositors observe the came with the heads: going armed with his brethren. The expositors observe the self and duty joined: look on the things of others (Phil. 2:4). The believer is taught to mark the the first part, the chose for himself, the came with the heads, the self and duty joined, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that Gad chose the first part yet went armed to execute God's justice with his brethren — self-interest rightly ordered to duty, bearing his part in the common cause though his own was secured (Phil. 2:4).
22:
"And of Dan he said, Dan is a lion's whelp: he shall leap from Bashan." The witnesses note the blessing of Dan — a lion's whelp, leaping from Bashan, courageous in war. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses said of the tribe of Dan. The witnesses note that Henry notes that, where Jacob compared Dan to a serpent for subtlety, Moses compares him to a lion for courage and resolution. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a lion's whelp — courage and resolution; the leap from Bashan — the bold defender; the lion not serpent — valour as well as craft (Henry); the boldness God's gift — the righteous bold as a lion (Prov. 28:1).

The puritan expositor (Henry) marks the two-sided portrait of Dan: Jacob had likened him to a "serpent" for subtlety, but Moses now to a "lion's whelp" for courage and resolution, "leaping from Bashan"; the change of figure shows the tribe's valour as well as its craft, a fierce and bold defender; the lion-courage given to Dan teaches that God endows his people with the boldness needed for their warfare, strength and resolution his gift; it bids the faithful be "bold as a lion" (Prov. 28:1) in the cause of God, Dan blessed with a lion's heart.

So the witnesses make the verse Dan a lion's whelp: The witnesses note the a lion's whelp: courage and resolution. The witnesses note the leap from Bashan: the bold defender. The witnesses note the lion not serpent: valour as well as craft (Henry). The witnesses note the boldness God's gift: the righteous bold as a lion (Prov. 28:1). The expositors observe the a lion's whelp: courage and resolution. The expositors observe the leap from Bashan: the bold defender. The expositors observe the lion not serpent: valour as well as craft (Henry). The expositors observe the boldness God's gift: the righteous bold as a lion (Prov. 28:1). The believer is taught to mark the a lion's whelp, the leap from Bashan, the lion not serpent, the boldness God's gift, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that Dan is a lion's whelp leaping from Bashan — courage and resolution God's gift, the boldness needed for his people's warfare, the righteous bold as a lion (Prov. 28:1).
23:
"And of Naphtali he said, O Naphtali, satisfied with favour, and full with the blessing of the LORD: possess thou the west and the south." The witnesses note the blessing of Naphtali — satisfied with favour and full of the LORD's blessing. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses said of the tribe of Naphtali. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Naphtali satisfied with favour, full of the LORD's blessing, possessing the west and the south. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the satisfied with favour — wanting nothing; the full of the blessing — the LORD's goodness; the possess west and south — a goodly portion; the favour the true satisfaction — satisfied with thy goodness (Ps. 65:4).

The witnesses agree the blessing of Naphtali is one of fulness and contentment in God's favour: "satisfied with favour, and full with the blessing of the LORD"; the tribe is pictured as wanting nothing, its cup full of God's goodness, and given a goodly portion of the land; the satisfaction "with favour" marks the deepest blessing — not bare possessions but the favour of God that fills and contents the soul; it teaches that the favour of the LORD is the true satisfaction of his people, "thou shalt be satisfied... with the goodness of thy house" (Ps. 65:4), Naphtali full and content in the blessing of God.

So the witnesses make the verse satisfied with favour: The witnesses note the satisfied with favour: wanting nothing. The witnesses note the full of the blessing: the LORD's goodness. The witnesses note the possess west and south: a goodly portion. The witnesses note the favour the true satisfaction: satisfied with thy goodness (Ps. 65:4). The expositors observe the satisfied with favour: wanting nothing. The expositors observe the full of the blessing: the LORD's goodness. The expositors observe the possess west and south: a goodly portion. The expositors observe the favour the true satisfaction: satisfied with thy goodness (Ps. 65:4). The believer is taught to mark the satisfied with favour, the full of the blessing, the possess west and south, the favour the true satisfaction, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that Naphtali is satisfied with favour and full of the LORD's blessing — the favour of God the true satisfaction of his people, the soul filled and content in his goodness (Ps. 65:4).
24:
"And of Asher he said, Let Asher be blessed with children; let him be acceptable to his brethren, and let him dip his foot in oil." The witnesses note the blessing of Asher — blessed with children, accepted of his brethren, his land rich in oil. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses said of the tribe of Asher. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Asher blessed with children, acceptable to his brethren, dipping his foot in oil. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the blessed with children — a numerous tribe; the acceptable to his brethren — well-beloved; the dip his foot in oil — rich in olives; the abundance and joy — my cup runneth over (Ps. 23:5).

The witnesses agree the blessing of Asher is one of plenty and favour — a numerous, well-liked tribe in a rich land: "blessed with children... acceptable to his brethren... dip his foot in oil"; the figure of dipping the foot in oil marks the abundance of olives in Asher's portion, so plentiful as to bathe the feet; the joining of fruitful family, the goodwill of the brethren, and material plenty pictures a full and happy estate; it teaches that God blesses his people with increase, favour, and abundance, the oil a token of richness and joy, "thou anointest my head with oil; my cup runneth over" (Ps. 23:5), Asher rich and well-beloved.

So the witnesses make the verse dip his foot in oil: The witnesses note the blessed with children: a numerous tribe. The witnesses note the acceptable to his brethren: well-beloved. The witnesses note the dip his foot in oil: rich in olives. The witnesses note the abundance and joy: my cup runneth over (Ps. 23:5). The expositors observe the blessed with children: a numerous tribe. The expositors observe the acceptable to his brethren: well-beloved. The expositors observe the dip his foot in oil: rich in olives. The expositors observe the abundance and joy: my cup runneth over (Ps. 23:5). The believer is taught to mark the blessed with children, the acceptable to his brethren, the dip his foot in oil, the abundance and joy, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that Asher is blessed with children, favour, and oil — increase, goodwill, and abundance, the oil a token of richness and joy, the full cup of God's blessing (Ps. 23:5).
25:
"Thy shoes shall be iron and brass; and as thy days, so shall thy strength be." The witnesses note Asher's strength and security, with the great promise — as thy days, so shall thy strength be. The witnesses note that Gill notes the shoes of iron and brass denote either abundance of those metals or a sure footing and defence; the strength promised to equal the days. The witnesses note that as his days, so should his strength be. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the shoes of iron and brass — a firm footing and defence; the as thy days — to match each day's need; the so shall thy strength be — grace measured to the day; the sufficient grace — my grace is sufficient (2 Cor. 12:9).

The witnesses agree the verse closes Asher's blessing with a promise dear to all the faithful: "as thy days, so shall thy strength be"; the iron and brass shoes speak of a firm footing and lasting defence, and the great word assures that strength shall be measured out to match each day's need; the promise teaches that God will give grace sufficient for every day, no trial outrunning the strength he supplies, "my grace is sufficient for thee" (2 Cor. 12:9); the believer's comfort is that as the days come, so will the strength, the supply renewed to the need, Asher shod with iron and upheld day by day.

So the witnesses make the verse as thy days thy strength: The witnesses note the shoes of iron and brass: a firm footing and defence. The witnesses note the as thy days: to match each day's need. The witnesses note the so shall thy strength be: grace measured to the day. The witnesses note the sufficient grace: my grace is sufficient (2 Cor. 12:9). The expositors observe the shoes of iron and brass: a firm footing and defence. The expositors observe the as thy days: to match each day's need. The expositors observe the so shall thy strength be: grace measured to the day. The expositors observe the sufficient grace: my grace is sufficient (2 Cor. 12:9). The believer is taught to mark the shoes of iron and brass, the as thy days, the so shall thy strength be, the sufficient grace, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that Asher is shod with iron and promised "as thy days, so shall thy strength be" — God giving grace sufficient for every day, no trial outrunning his supply, his grace sufficient (2 Cor. 12:9).
26:
"There is none like unto the God of Jeshurun, who rideth upon the heaven in thy help, and in his excellency on the sky." The witnesses note the incomparable God of Jeshurun, who rides the heavens to help his people. The witnesses note that Gill notes Jeshurun is Israel; the Lord was their God in a special sense, and there is none like him for the perfection of his being. The witnesses note that he rides upon the heaven in their help. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the none like the God of Jeshurun — the incomparable LORD; the rideth upon the heaven — the heavens marshalled; the in thy help — ready to save; the an almighty helper — who is like unto thee? (Exod. 15:11).

The witnesses agree the closing benediction rises to the praise of the incomparable God who fights for his own: "there is none like unto the God of Jeshurun, who rideth upon the heaven in thy help"; no god, no power, is like the LORD, who marshals the very heavens to aid his people; the figure of riding the sky in their help magnifies his majesty and his readiness to save; it teaches that the people of God have an almighty and unrivalled helper, who employs the whole creation for their good, "who is like unto thee, O LORD... glorious in holiness?" (Exod. 15:11), the God of Jeshurun riding to their rescue.

So the witnesses make the verse none like God: The witnesses note the none like the God of Jeshurun: the incomparable LORD. The witnesses note the rideth upon the heaven: the heavens marshalled. The witnesses note the in thy help: ready to save. The witnesses note the an almighty helper: who is like unto thee? (Exod. 15:11). The expositors observe the none like the God of Jeshurun: the incomparable LORD. The expositors observe the rideth upon the heaven: the heavens marshalled. The expositors observe the in thy help: ready to save. The expositors observe the an almighty helper: who is like unto thee? (Exod. 15:11). The believer is taught to mark the none like the God of Jeshurun, the rideth upon the heaven, the in thy help, the an almighty helper, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that none is like the God of Jeshurun, who rides the heaven to help his people — the incomparable LORD, an almighty helper employing all creation for their good, who is like unto thee (Exod. 15:11).
27:
"The eternal God is thy refuge, and underneath are the everlasting arms: and he shall thrust out the enemy from before thee; and shall say, Destroy them." The witnesses note the great comfort — the eternal God a refuge, the everlasting arms underneath his people. The witnesses note that Gill notes God is eternal, from everlasting to everlasting, the Ancient of days; the same true of Christ, the everlasting Father, the true God and eternal life. The witnesses note that the everlasting arms are underneath, and he thrusts out the enemy. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the eternal God — from everlasting to everlasting; the thy refuge — the shelter of his people; the the everlasting arms — underneath, never failing; the upheld from beneath — the LORD my high tower (Ps. 18:2).

The witnesses agree this verse is one of the great comforts of all Scripture: "the eternal God is thy refuge, and underneath are the everlasting arms"; the people of God have for their shelter the everlasting God himself, and for their support arms that never fail nor grow weary, bearing them up from beneath; Gill applies "the eternal God" to Christ, the everlasting Father, the true God and eternal life; it teaches that the faithful are upheld by an almighty and unfailing strength, never let fall, "underneath are the everlasting arms" — a refuge above and a support below, "the LORD is... my high tower" (Ps. 18:2), the eternal God their dwelling-place.

So the witnesses make the verse the everlasting arms: The witnesses note the the eternal God: from everlasting to everlasting. The witnesses note the thy refuge: the shelter of his people. The witnesses note the the everlasting arms: underneath, never failing. The witnesses note the upheld from beneath: the LORD my high tower (Ps. 18:2). The expositors observe the the eternal God: from everlasting to everlasting. The expositors observe the thy refuge: the shelter of his people. The expositors observe the the everlasting arms: underneath, never failing. The expositors observe the upheld from beneath: the LORD my high tower (Ps. 18:2). The believer is taught to mark the the eternal God, the thy refuge, the the everlasting arms, the upheld from beneath, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that the eternal God is their refuge and the everlasting arms underneath — the people of God upheld by an almighty, unfailing strength, a refuge above and support below, the eternal God their dwelling-place (Ps. 18:2).
28:
"Israel then shall dwell in safety alone: the fountain of Jacob shall be upon a land of corn and wine; also his heavens shall drop down dew." The witnesses note Israel dwelling in safety, set apart, in a land of corn and wine. The witnesses note that Gill notes Israel, the Canaanites being thrust out, should dwell in safety or confidently, without fear, though alone, a separate and distinct people. The witnesses note that the land yielded corn and wine and the heavens dropped dew. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the dwell in safety — the enemy thrust out; the alone — a separate, distinct people; the corn and wine — the bounty of the land; the safe in distinctness — the people dwell alone (Num. 23:9).

The witnesses agree the blessing promises a secure and abundant dwelling for a separated people: "Israel then shall dwell in safety alone... a land of corn and wine... his heavens shall drop down dew"; their safety lay in being God's peculiar people, set apart from the nations, and their plenty in the bounty of the land; the dwelling "alone" — distinct from all others — marks both their separation and their security under God; it teaches that the people of God are kept safe in their distinctness from the world, "the people shall dwell alone, and shall not be reckoned among the nations" (Num. 23:9), safe and supplied under his care.

So the witnesses make the verse dwell in safety: The witnesses note the dwell in safety: the enemy thrust out. The witnesses note the alone: a separate, distinct people. The witnesses note the corn and wine: the bounty of the land. The witnesses note the safe in distinctness: the people dwell alone (Num. 23:9). The expositors observe the dwell in safety: the enemy thrust out. The expositors observe the alone: a separate, distinct people. The expositors observe the corn and wine: the bounty of the land. The expositors observe the safe in distinctness: the people dwell alone (Num. 23:9). The believer is taught to mark the dwell in safety, the alone, the corn and wine, the safe in distinctness, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that Israel dwells in safety alone, in a land of corn and wine — a separated people kept secure under God, safe in their distinctness from the world, dwelling alone (Num. 23:9).
29:
"Happy art thou, O Israel: who is like unto thee, O people saved by the LORD, the shield of thy help, and who is the sword of thy excellency!..." The witnesses note the closing benediction — the happiness of Israel, a people saved by the LORD their shield and sword. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is the conclusion of the blessing, summed up in a few words — Israel happy in having God for their God and so many good things promised. The witnesses note that they were a people saved by the LORD, his shield and sword. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the happy art thou — the blessedness of Israel; the saved by the LORD — their salvation his; the the shield and sword — defence and triumph; the God their God — happy the people whose God is the LORD (Ps. 144:15).

The witnesses agree the Song of blessing ends in a burst of joy over the blessedness of God's people: "happy art thou, O Israel: who is like unto thee, O people saved by the LORD"; their happiness lies in this above all — that the LORD himself is their salvation, "the shield of thy help, and the sword of thy excellency," both their defence and their offence; no people on earth is like them, for they have God for their own; it teaches that the true blessedness of any people is to have the LORD for their God and Saviour, "happy is that people, whose God is the LORD" (Ps. 144:15), saved by him, shielded and made to triumph — a fitting close to the blessing of Moses.

So the witnesses make the verse happy art thou: The witnesses note the happy art thou: the blessedness of Israel. The witnesses note the saved by the LORD: their salvation his. The witnesses note the the shield and sword: defence and triumph. The witnesses note the God their God: happy the people whose God is the LORD (Ps. 144:15). The expositors observe the happy art thou: the blessedness of Israel. The expositors observe the saved by the LORD: their salvation his. The expositors observe the the shield and sword: defence and triumph. The expositors observe the God their God: happy the people whose God is the LORD (Ps. 144:15). The believer is taught to mark the happy art thou, the saved by the LORD, the the shield and sword, the God their God, and to rest in the everlasting arms. The verse thus records that Israel is happy, a people saved by the LORD their shield and sword — the true blessedness of God's people to have him for their God and Saviour, happy the people whose God is the LORD (Ps. 144:15).
